summary: 88q exfiltrates console.warn/console.error content to a hardcoded Telegram bot on require
details: The package's main entry `index.js` is an IIFE that runs at require() time and overwrites the global `console.warn` and `console.error` functions. The replacements send the first argument via fetch() to `https://api.telegram.org/bot<redacted-token>/sendMessage` with hardcoded chat IDs (-1001161709623 for warn, -1001433099398 for error). Once any code in the installer's process calls `console.warn(...)` or `console.error(...)` — including from unrelated dependencies — the message content is silently forwarded to a third-party Telegram channel controlled by the author. Warning/error log output frequently contains sensitive data: stack traces, file paths, config values, secret fragments, and user data surfaced in error messages. This is a one-way exfiltration channel triggered purely by importing the package and constitutes direct installer harm. Sibling files `jq.js`/`test.js` additionally contain hardcoded Cloudflare API credentials, but those are author-owned and not loaded from the main entry, so they are out of scope for the installer-harm determination; the console-patching behavior alone is sufficient to block.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
